title: From High School Coach to Tech CEO: The Accidental Entrepreneur's Journey
guestBio: Alyssa Nolte is the CEO of TruVue and host of two podcasts: The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday. She's an accidental entrepreneur who built her tech company after starting as a volunteer dance coach, and she's passionate about helping women balance leadership with motherhood.
excerpt: What if your leadership story didn't start in a boardroom, but on a high school gym floor? Alyssa Nolte shares how she accidentally built a tech company while coaching teenagers and why starting before you're ready might be exactly what you need.
metaDescription: Discover how Alyssa Nolte went from volunteer dance coach to tech CEO, plus insights on balancing motherhood with entrepreneurship.
keyTakeaways: Leadership skills can be developed in unexpected places, including volunteer coaching positions.
Starting before you feel qualified is often the key to entrepreneurial success.
Invisible labor in motherhood translates directly to valuable leadership skills.
Systems thinking and intuition can successfully coexist in business leadership.
Humor and authenticity are powerful tools for leading through uncertainty.
Women need to own and recognize their leadership abilities instead of downplaying them.
Real-world problem solving often leads to the most successful business ventures.

pullQuotes.text: Invisible labor IS leadership and women need to own it

timestamps.label: From dance coach to tech founder
The origin story of Form Dash and TruVue
Navigating leadership as a mom and CEO
Using humor and intuition in leadership

content:
Have you ever looked around at successful entrepreneurs and wondered if you have what it takes? Maybe you've told yourself you need more experience, more credentials, or more time before you can pursue that business idea that keeps nagging at you. If so, you're going to love hearing from Alyssa Nolte, CEO of TruVue and host of two incredibly successful podcasts.

Alyssa's story is proof that leadership doesn't always emerge from traditional boardrooms or MBA programs. Sometimes it starts on a high school gym floor, coaching teenagers through dance routines and learning to solve problems with heart, humor, and sheer determination.

When I first met Alyssa on a Viva Women's Roundtable hosted by Midori Verity, we instantly connected over our shared experiences as women navigating the tech world while raising young children. What struck me most about her wasn't just her business acumen, but her ability to blend systems thinking with genuine intuition in a way that feels both strategic and deeply human.

The Unexpected Path to Tech Leadership

Alyssa's journey from volunteer dance coach to tech CEO isn't just inspiring, it's instructive. Her transition demonstrates something crucial that many women don't recognize about themselves: you're already developing leadership skills in ways you might not even realize. Every time you coordinate schedules, manage competing priorities, or motivate others to achieve their best, you're practicing the exact skills that successful entrepreneurs use every day.

The creation of Form Dash and eventually TruVue didn't happen because Alyssa woke up one day deciding to become a tech founder. It happened because she saw real problems that needed solving and refused to accept that someone else would have to fix them. This is the essence of accidental entrepreneurship, and it's often the most authentic path to building something meaningful.

What makes Alyssa's approach particularly powerful is her recognition that traditional business wisdom doesn't always apply to women, especially mothers. She's learned to trust her instincts while building robust systems, creating a leadership style that's both data-driven and deeply intuitive.

Redefining Leadership Through Motherhood

One of the most important conversations we had centered around the concept of invisible labor and its connection to leadership. As mothers, we're constantly managing complex systems, anticipating needs, and making decisions that keep entire households functioning smoothly. Yet somehow, we've been conditioned to view this work as just parenting rather than recognizing it as sophisticated leadership in action.

Alyssa challenges this narrative head-on. She sees the project management skills required to coordinate family schedules, the emotional intelligence needed to motivate children through difficult moments, and the strategic thinking involved in long-term family planning as direct preparation for business leadership. More importantly, she's learned to articulate these skills in professional contexts rather than minimizing them.

This reframe is crucial because it gives you permission to see yourself as already qualified for leadership roles you might have been hesitating to pursue. You don't need to wait until your children are older or until you have more traditional business experience. The leadership muscles you're building right now are real, valuable, and transferable.

Leading with Humor and Authenticity

What sets Alyssa apart in the tech world is her willingness to bring her whole self to her leadership role, including her sense of humor and her authentic personality. In industries that often prioritize appearing serious and having all the answers, she's discovered that vulnerability and humor can actually be competitive advantages.

This approach resonates particularly well with teams and customers because it creates psychological safety. When leaders are willing to acknowledge uncertainty and find lightness in challenging situations, it gives everyone permission to be more creative, take appropriate risks, and recover more quickly from setbacks.

Through her experience hosting both The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday, Alyssa has learned that different audiences connect with different aspects of authentic leadership. This multi-faceted approach to sharing her expertise demonstrates another important principle: you don't have to fit into a single box or serve just one type of audience to build a successful platform.

Starting Before You're Ready

Perhaps the most actionable insight from our conversation is Alyssa's emphasis on starting before you feel completely prepared. This isn't about being reckless or unprofessional. It's about recognizing that readiness is often an illusion, and that waiting for perfect conditions usually means waiting forever.

The problems you can see clearly right now, the solutions that seem obvious to you, and the improvements you know you could make in your industry are all valid starting points for meaningful work. You don't need someone else's permission to begin addressing these challenges, and you don't need to have every detail figured out before you take the first step.

What you do need is the willingness to learn as you go, the courage to iterate based on feedback, and the persistence to keep moving forward even when the path isn't entirely clear. These are skills that most women, especially mothers, already possess in abundance.

Alyssa's story reminds us that accidental entrepreneurs often build the most resilient businesses because they're driven by genuine problem-solving rather than abstract ambition. If you've been waiting for the right time, the right credentials, or the right opportunity to start that project that keeps calling to you, maybe it's time to trust that your current experience has already prepared you more than you realize.

The leadership qualities you're developing every day, the problems you're uniquely positioned to solve, and the perspective you bring as a woman navigating multiple roles aren't obstacles to overcome. They're the foundation for the meaningful work you're meant to do.
